model.caldata_engaged_california.int_e3_.raw_code:
"""dbt Python model that labels topics generated by int_e3_topic_modeling using Snowflake Cortex.

This script aggregates per-topic examples from the topic modeling output, samples up to ten
high-probability comments per topic, concatenates them, and sends batched requests to the
Cortex COMPLETE function for LLM-based topic labeling. The JSON responses are parsed and
stored for downstream consumption.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from snowflake.snowpark import Session

MAX_COMMENTS_PER_TOPIC = 10
SNIPPET_CHAR_LIMIT = 500

RESPONSE_FORMAT_JSON = (
    '{"type":"json","schema":{"type":"object",'
    '"properties":{'
    '"title":{"type":"string"},'
    '"description":{"type":"string"},'
    '"quotes":{"type":"array","items":{"type":"string"}}'
    '},'
    '"required":["title","description","quotes"]}}'
)
RESPONSE_FORMAT_LITERAL = f"PARSE_JSON('{RESPONSE_FORMAT_JSON}')"

PROMPT_HEADER = (
    "The following are ideas from California state employees to improve the efficiency of the california state government. "
    "The ideas represent a single topic or theme. The ideas may include specific problems and solutions. "
    "Analyze the items and return a JSON object with: \n"
    "- `title`: a concise 2-6 word topic title that avoids generic terms like government, efficiency, streamline, engage, or improve. The title should be as specific as possible, yet still capture the essence of all ideas presented.\n"
    "- `description`: no more than three sentences summarizing the common theme; focus on specific problems and solutions.\n"
    "- `quotes`: an array of 2-3 representative quotes (each about one sentence) drawn from the content. Quotes may use ellipses or bracketed clarifications but must stay true to the source.\n\n"
    "Employee efficiency ideas in this theme: "
)


def resolve_llm_model(dbt) -> str:
    """Pull the configured LLM model name from dbt meta config."""
    meta = dbt.config.get("meta") or {}
    llm_model = meta.get("llm_model", "")
    if isinstance(llm_model, str) and llm_model.strip():
        return llm_model.strip()
    raise ValueError(
        "llm_model not found in model meta config. "
        "Ensure it is set in schema.yml under config.meta."
    )


def model(dbt, session: Session):
    """Main dbt entrypoint for topic labeling."""

    dbt.config(materialized="table", packages=[])

    if dbt.this.database:
        session.use_database(dbt.this.database)
    if dbt.this.schema:
        session.use_schema(dbt.this.schema)

    modeling_df = dbt.ref("int_e3_topic_modeling")
    modeling_df.create_or_replace_temp_view("topic_modeling")

    llm_model = resolve_llm_model(dbt)
    prompt_header_safe = PROMPT_HEADER.replace("'", "''")

    # Sample up to ten high-probability comments per topic, concatenate them, and send a single batched
    # request to the Cortex COMPLETE function. The JSON output is stored for downstream consumption.
    sql = f"""
    with ranked as (
        select
            content_type,
            topic_id,
            original_text,
            topic_probability,
            content_id,
            row_number() over (
                partition by content_type, topic_id
                order by topic_probability desc nulls last, content_id
            ) as rn,
            count(*) over (partition by content_type, topic_id) as total_count
        from topic_modeling
        where topic_id is not null
    ),
    trimmed as (
        select
            content_type,
            topic_id,
            substr(original_text, 1, {SNIPPET_CHAR_LIMIT}) as snippet,
            rn,
            total_count
        from ranked
        where rn <= {MAX_COMMENTS_PER_TOPIC}
    ),
    aggregated as (
        select
            content_type,
            topic_id,
            max(total_count) as topic_member_count,
            listagg(snippet, '; ')
                within group (order by rn) as comments_for_labeling
        from trimmed
        group by content_type, topic_id
    ),
    labeled as (
        select
            content_type,
            topic_id,
            topic_member_count,
            comments_for_labeling,
            AI_COMPLETE(
                model => '{llm_model}',
                prompt => '{prompt_header_safe}' || replace(coalesce(comments_for_labeling, ''), '''', ''''''),
                model_parameters => OBJECT_CONSTRUCT('temperature', 0.01),
                response_format => {RESPONSE_FORMAT_LITERAL}
            ) as raw_response
        from aggregated
    ),
    parsed as (
        select
            content_type,
            topic_id,
            topic_member_count,
            comments_for_labeling,
            try_parse_json(raw_response) as parsed_response
        from labeled
    )
    select
        content_type,
        topic_id,
        topic_member_count,
        coalesce(parsed_response:"title"::string, concat('Topic ', topic_id)) as topic_name,
        coalesce(parsed_response:"description"::string, 'No description available') as topic_description,
        coalesce(to_json(parsed_response:"quotes"), '[]') as representative_quotes,
        comments_for_labeling
    from parsed
    """

    return session.sql(sql)
model.caldata_engaged_california.int_e3_.compiled_code:
"""dbt Python model that labels topics generated by int_e3_topic_modeling using Snowflake Cortex.

This script aggregates per-topic examples from the topic modeling output, samples up to ten
high-probability comments per topic, concatenates them, and sends batched requests to the
Cortex COMPLETE function for LLM-based topic labeling. The JSON responses are parsed and
stored for downstream consumption.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from snowflake.snowpark import Session

MAX_COMMENTS_PER_TOPIC = 10
SNIPPET_CHAR_LIMIT = 500

RESPONSE_FORMAT_JSON = (
    '{"type":"json","schema":{"type":"object",'
    '"properties":{'
    '"title":{"type":"string"},'
    '"description":{"type":"string"},'
    '"quotes":{"type":"array","items":{"type":"string"}}'
    '},'
    '"required":["title","description","quotes"]}}'
)
RESPONSE_FORMAT_LITERAL = f"PARSE_JSON('{RESPONSE_FORMAT_JSON}')"

PROMPT_HEADER = (
    "The following are ideas from California state employees to improve the efficiency of the california state government. "
    "The ideas represent a single topic or theme. The ideas may include specific problems and solutions. "
    "Analyze the items and return a JSON object with: \n"
    "- `title`: a concise 2-6 word topic title that avoids generic terms like government, efficiency, streamline, engage, or improve. The title should be as specific as possible, yet still capture the essence of all ideas presented.\n"
    "- `description`: no more than three sentences summarizing the common theme; focus on specific problems and solutions.\n"
    "- `quotes`: an array of 2-3 representative quotes (each about one sentence) drawn from the content. Quotes may use ellipses or bracketed clarifications but must stay true to the source.\n\n"
    "Employee efficiency ideas in this theme: "
)


def resolve_llm_model(dbt) -> str:
    """Pull the configured LLM model name from dbt meta config."""
    meta = dbt.config.get("meta") or {}
    llm_model = meta.get("llm_model", "")
    if isinstance(llm_model, str) and llm_model.strip():
        return llm_model.strip()
    raise ValueError(
        "llm_model not found in model meta config. "
        "Ensure it is set in schema.yml under config.meta."
    )


def model(dbt, session: Session):
    """Main dbt entrypoint for topic labeling."""

    dbt.config(materialized="table", packages=[])

    if dbt.this.database:
        session.use_database(dbt.this.database)
    if dbt.this.schema:
        session.use_schema(dbt.this.schema)

    modeling_df = dbt.ref("int_e3_topic_modeling")
    modeling_df.create_or_replace_temp_view("topic_modeling")

    llm_model = resolve_llm_model(dbt)
    prompt_header_safe = PROMPT_HEADER.replace("'", "''")

    # Sample up to ten high-probability comments per topic, concatenate them, and send a single batched
    # request to the Cortex COMPLETE function. The JSON output is stored for downstream consumption.
    sql = f"""
    with ranked as (
        select
            content_type,
            topic_id,
            original_text,
            topic_probability,
            content_id,
            row_number() over (
                partition by content_type, topic_id
                order by topic_probability desc nulls last, content_id
            ) as rn,
            count(*) over (partition by content_type, topic_id) as total_count
        from topic_modeling
        where topic_id is not null
    ),
    trimmed as (
        select
            content_type,
            topic_id,
            substr(original_text, 1, {SNIPPET_CHAR_LIMIT}) as snippet,
            rn,
            total_count
        from ranked
        where rn <= {MAX_COMMENTS_PER_TOPIC}
    ),
    aggregated as (
        select
            content_type,
            topic_id,
            max(total_count) as topic_member_count,
            listagg(snippet, '; ')
                within group (order by rn) as comments_for_labeling
        from trimmed
        group by content_type, topic_id
    ),
    labeled as (
        select
            content_type,
            topic_id,
            topic_member_count,
            comments_for_labeling,
            AI_COMPLETE(
                model => '{llm_model}',
                prompt => '{prompt_header_safe}' || replace(coalesce(comments_for_labeling, ''), '''', ''''''),
                model_parameters => OBJECT_CONSTRUCT('temperature', 0.01),
                response_format => {RESPONSE_FORMAT_LITERAL}
            ) as raw_response
        from aggregated
    ),
    parsed as (
        select
            content_type,
            topic_id,
            topic_member_count,
            comments_for_labeling,
            try_parse_json(raw_response) as parsed_response
        from labeled
    )
    select
        content_type,
        topic_id,
        topic_member_count,
        coalesce(parsed_response:"title"::string, concat('Topic ', topic_id)) as topic_name,
        coalesce(parsed_response:"description"::string, 'No description available') as topic_description,
        coalesce(to_json(parsed_response:"quotes"), '[]') as representative_quotes,
        comments_for_labeling
    from parsed
    """

    return session.sql(sql)


# This part is user provided model code
# you will need to copy the next section to run the code
# COMMAND ----------
# this part is dbt logic for get ref work, do not modify

def ref(*args, **kwargs):
    refs = {"int_e3_topic_modeling": "TRANSFORM_ENGCA_PRD.ethelo_e3.int_e3_topic_modeling"}
    key = '.'.join(args)
    version = kwargs.get("v") or kwargs.get("version")
    if version:
        key += f".v{version}"
    dbt_load_df_function = kwargs.get("dbt_load_df_function")
    return dbt_load_df_function(refs[key])


def source(*args, dbt_load_df_function):
    sources = {}
    key = '.'.join(args)
    return dbt_load_df_function(sources[key])


config_dict = {'meta': {'llm_model': 'claude-4-sonnet'}}


class config:
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        pass

    @staticmethod
    def get(key, default=None):
        return config_dict.get(key, default)

class this:
    """dbt.this() or dbt.this.identifier"""
    database = "TRANSFORM_ENGCA_PRD"
    schema = "ethelo_e3"
    identifier = "int_e3_topic_labeling"
    
    def __repr__(self):
        return 'TRANSFORM_ENGCA_PRD.ethelo_e3.int_e3_topic_labeling'


class dbtObj:
    def __init__(self, load_df_function) -> None:
        self.source = lambda *args: source(*args, dbt_load_df_function=load_df_function)
        self.ref = lambda *args, **kwargs: ref(*args, **kwargs, dbt_load_df_function=load_df_function)
        self.config = config
        self.this = this()
        self.is_incremental = False

source.caldata_engaged_california.UX_AND.name: E3_HAND_LABELLED_MAIN_IDEAS
source.caldata_engaged_california.UX_AND.source_description:
Data received from the UX and Research team. Includes hand-labels and QA data.
You may see references to E3 in this directory. This is the internal naming convention our team used
to refer to the State Employees engagement. It refers to the "efficient, effective, and engaged" language
in the Executive Order N-30-25.
source.caldata_engaged_california.UX_AND.description:
Around 400 Main Idea responses from the E3 platform that are hand labelled by the UX and
research team with the following properties:
- Main Idea Type is a category that filters ideas for whether they are a good target for
  efficiencies ideas or are too braod
- Main Idea Primary Theme is a list of curated themes that buckets each comment into
  1 or more topical theme.

The original table this was pulled from to provide to the team is the E3_PARTICIPANT_RESPONSES
mart model.

MAIN_IDEA.name: MAIN_IDEA
MAIN_IDEA.description: Content of the response to the Main Idea question.

MAIN_IDEA_TYPE.name: MAIN_IDEA_TYPE
MAIN_IDEA_TYPE.description: Label for what 'type' of response this is.

MAIN_IDEA_PRIMARY_THEME.name: MAIN_IDEA_PRIMARY_THEME
MAIN_IDEA_PRIMARY_THEME.description: Theme(s) that apply to each response.

REVIEWER.name: REVIEWER
REVIEWER.description: ODI staff member who created the labelling

You may see references to E3 in this directory. This is the internal naming convention our team used
to refer to the State Employees engagement. It refers to the "efficient, effective, and engaged" language
in the Executive Order N-30-25.
source.caldata_engaged_california.UX_AND.description:
Mapping table of e3 themes to subthemes. One row per subtheme. Defined by data engineering team.
Developed by iterative pruning and refining of the subthemes provided by the UX and Research team.

MAIN_THEME.description: Comment main themes identified by UX and Research team.

SUBTHEME.name: SUBTHEME
SUBTHEME.description: Comment subthemes identified by data engineering team through iterative review of categories.

SUBTHEME_DESCRIPTION.description: Subtheme descriptions used to guide llm labelling of comments.

MAIN_THEME_ID.name: MAIN_THEME_ID
MAIN_THEME_ID.description: Unique identifier for each main theme.

source.caldata_engaged_california.LA_FIR.name: BUILDINGS_WITH_DINS_2023
source.caldata_engaged_california.LA_FIR.source_description: Source datasets for EngagedCA that are too large to be added as a seed file.
source.caldata_engaged_california.LA_FIR.description:
LA geographic data from
https://geohub.lacity.org/datasets/lacounty::2023-buildings-with-dins-data-/explore.
This GIS dataset contains building footprints depicting building shape and location
in select portions of Los
Angeles County, with DINS data as of January 27, 2025 from the Palisades and Eaton
Fires attached to the building
footprint. Building footprints with 1/4 mile of the Eaton and Palisades Fire Perimeters
are included, but may not
have DAMAGE information. HEIGHT is the height of the building and ELEV is the elevation
of the roof (elevation of
footprint + height).

macro.caldata_engaged_california.drop_sc.name: drop_schemas_with_prefix
macro.caldata_engaged_california.drop_sc.description:
Macro to drop all schemas in the database with a specified prefix.

This macro constructs the schema name using the provided prefix and drops
it if it exists.
Useful for cleaning up test or temporary schemas in CI/CD workflows.

arguments.name: prefix
arguments.description: The prefix to use when constructing the schema name to drop.

arguments.name: database
arguments.description: The database in which the schema resides. Optional; defaults to the target database.

macro.caldata_engaged_california.generat.name: generate_schema_name
macro.caldata_engaged_california.generat.description:
Macro to retrieve a custom schema name for a model or source.

This macro returns the schema name based on project conventions,
environment, or other logic.
Useful for dynamically assigning schemas in multi-environment dbt projects.

arguments.name: model_name
arguments.description: The name of the model or source for which to get the custom schema.

arguments.name: environment
arguments.description:
The environment (e.g., dev, prod) to use when determining the schema name.
Optional.

macro.caldata_engaged_california._get_al.name: _get_all_table_columns

macro.caldata_engaged_california._valida.name: _validate_single_table_schema

macro.caldata_engaged_california.validat.name: validate_all_schemas
macro.caldata_engaged_california.validat.description:
Macro to validate all model and source schemas in the project against
their documentation.

This macro creates a comprehensive report of schema validation issues
across all models and sources in the project. It compares the documented
columns in dbt YAML files with the actual columns in the database
tables/views.

The macro will always raise an error if validation issues are found, making
it suitable for use in CI/CD pipelines to enforce schema documentation
standards.

arguments.name: errors_only
arguments.description:
If true, only shows tables with validation errors. If false, shows all
validation results including successful matches. Defaults to false.

arguments.name: undocumented_columns_as_errors
arguments.description:
If true, treats undocumented columns as validation errors. If false,
undocumented columns are reported as warnings but don't cause failure.
Defaults to true.

snowflake__get_catalog_tables_sql
macro.dbt_snowflake.snowflake__get_catal.macro_sql:
{% macro snowflake__get_catalog_tables_sql(information_schema) -%}
    select
        table_catalog as "table_database",
        table_schema as "table_schema",
        table_name as "table_name",
        case
            when is_dynamic = 'YES' and table_type = 'BASE TABLE' THEN 'DYNAMIC TABLE'
            else table_type
        end as "table_type",
        comment as "table_comment",

        -- note: this is the _role_ that owns the table
        table_owner as "table_owner",

        'Clustering Key' as "stats:clustering_key:label",
        clustering_key as "stats:clustering_key:value",
        'The key used to cluster this table' as "stats:clustering_key:description",
        (clustering_key is not null) as "stats:clustering_key:include",

        'Row Count' as "stats:row_count:label",
        row_count as "stats:row_count:value",
        'An approximate count of rows in this table' as "stats:row_count:description",
        (row_count is not null) as "stats:row_count:include",

        'Approximate Size' as "stats:bytes:label",
        bytes as "stats:bytes:value",
        'Approximate size of the table as reported by Snowflake' as "stats:bytes:description",
        (bytes is not null) as "stats:bytes:include",

        'Last Modified' as "stats:last_modified:label",
        to_varchar(convert_timezone('UTC', last_altered), 'yyyy-mm-dd HH24:MI'||'UTC') as "stats:last_modified:value",
        'The timestamp for last update/change' as "stats:last_modified:description",
        (last_altered is not null and table_type='BASE TABLE') as "stats:last_modified:include"
    from {{ information_schema }}.tables
{%- endmacro %}

macro.dbt_snowflake.snowflake__get_catal.name: snowflake__get_catalog_columns_sql
macro.dbt_snowflake.snowflake__get_catal.macro_sql:
{% macro snowflake__get_catalog_columns_sql(information_schema) -%}
    select
        table_catalog as "table_database",
        table_schema as "table_schema",
        table_name as "table_name",

        column_name as "column_name",
        ordinal_position as "column_index",
        data_type as "column_type",
        comment as "column_comment"
    from {{ information_schema }}.columns
{%- endmacro %}

doc.caldata_engaged_california.__overvie.name: __overview__
doc.caldata_engaged_california.__overvie.block_contents:
# Caldata Engaged California dbt Documentation

Welcome to Caldata Data Operations and Engineering's Caldata Engaged California `dbt` docs.
To go back to the top-level docs, follow [this link](../)

## Navigation

You can use the `Project` and `Database` navigation tabs on the left side of the window to explore the models in your project.

### Project Tab

The Project tab mirrors the directory structure of your dbt project.
In this tab, you can see all of the models defined in your dbt project, as well as models imported from dbt packages.

### Database Tab

The Database tab also exposes your models, but in a format that looks more like a database explorer.
This view shows relations (tables and views) grouped into database schemas.
Note that ephemeral models are not shown in this interface, as they do not exist in the database.

## Graph Exploration

You can click the blue icon on the bottom-right corner of the page to view the lineage graph of your models.

On model pages, you'll see the immediate parents and children of the model you're exploring.
By clicking the Expand button at the top-right of this lineage pane,
you'll be able to see all of the models that are used to build, or are built from,
the model you're exploring.

Once expanded, you'll be able to use the `--select` and `--exclude` model selection syntax to filter the models in the graph.
For more information on model selection, check out the [dbt docs](https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/syntax).

Note that you can also right-click on models to interactively filter and explore the graph.

doc.dbt.__overview__.block_contents:
### Welcome!

Welcome to the auto-generated documentation for your dbt project!

### Navigation

You can use the `Project` and `Database` navigation tabs on the left side of the window to explore the models
in your project.

#### Project Tab
The `Project` tab mirrors the directory structure of your dbt project. In this tab, you can see all of the
models defined in your dbt project, as well as models imported from dbt packages.

#### Database Tab
The `Database` tab also exposes your models, but in a format that looks more like a database explorer. This view
shows relations (tables and views) grouped into database schemas. Note that ephemeral models are _not_ shown
in this interface, as they do not exist in the database.

### Graph Exploration
You can click the blue icon on the bottom-right corner of the page to view the lineage graph of your models.

On model pages, you'll see the immediate parents and children of the model you're exploring. By clicking the `Expand`
button at the top-right of this lineage pane, you'll be able to see all of the models that are used to build,
or are built from, the model you're exploring.

Once expanded, you'll be able to use the `--select` and `--exclude` model selection syntax to filter the
models in the graph. For more information on model selection, check out the [dbt docs](https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/model-selection-syntax).

Note that you can also right-click on models to interactively filter and explore the graph.

---

### More information

- [What is dbt](https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/introduction)?
- Read the [dbt viewpoint](https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/viewpoint)
- [Installation](https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/installation)
- Join the [dbt Community](https://www.getdbt.com/community/) for questions and discussion
